analysis: Two-circuit (transactional vs broad money) divergence — grounded on real FRED/CFS data
question: Do the transactional (MT) and asset/broad circuits vary? Grounded across episodes.
artifact_free_measure: CFS Divisia M1 (continuous across the May-2020 M1 redefinition; user-cost weighted)
caveat_on_naive_MT: Naive currency+demand MT gave +113.9% for 2020-22 but is contaminated by the May-2020 M1 reclassification. Divisia M1 strips the artifact: true surge +63.8%.

key_finding_1_distinct_objects.reading: Circuits are NOT locked together; correlation 0.68 not ~1.0 means genuinely independent information.

key_finding_2_regime_flip.reading: In normal times M2 predicts inflation better and transactional tells you ~nothing; in HIGH-inflation regimes it flips and the transactional aggregate wins. Matches the framework's core claim and the composition-MT result (0.186 vs 0.043).

honest_limits: Divergence DIRECTION is episode-specific: transactional led in 2020-22 but M2 led in the 1970s. NOT 'transactional always runs hot.'
The defensible general claim is regime-conditional PREDICTIVE power, not a constant growth ratio.
MA (asset circuit) has no clean dollar measure here (Divisia is an index); only the relative-growth / residual direction is supportable.
Single-country, and the high-regime n is dominated by a few episodes.
verdict: Grounded YES: the two circuits demonstrably vary (corr 0.68), and the transactional aggregate out-predicts M2 for inflation specifically in high-inflation regimes (0.138 vs 0.075). The 2020-22 divergence is real and artifact-free (1.6x) but episode-specific in direction.
